passages.text: The Anthropocene is a proposed geological epoch dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems. (17) ---- scientists argue about the exact start date, many suggest the Industrial Revolution as the primary catalyst. This era (18) ---- a massive increase in carbon emissions, which (19) ---- altered the climate. Furthermore, the (20) ---- of plastics into the environment has left a permanent mark. Unless we (21) ---- our current trajectory, the ecological damage may become irreversible.

Quantum computing represents a paradigm shift in how information is processed. Unlike classical computers, (22) ---- use bits, quantum computers utilize qubits. These units (23) ---- to exist in multiple states simultaneously due to superposition. While the technology is still (24) ---- its infancy, it holds the potential to (25) ---- fields like cryptography and material science. Research (26) ---- extensively over the next decade to overcome current hardware limitations.

The advent of CRISPR-Cas9 technology has revolutionized the field of genetic engineering, offering unprecedented precision in gene editing. Unlike previous methods such as ZFNs or TALENs, CRISPR utilizes a guide RNA to direct the Cas9 enzyme to a specific genomic location. While this simplicity facilitates rapid research, it raises profound ethical concerns regarding 'off-target' effects where unintended mutations occur. Moreover, the potential for germline editing—altering the DNA of embryos—poses a risk of permanent changes to the human gene pool. Many bioethicists argue that without a global regulatory framework, the democratisation of this tool could lead to unforeseen ecological and societal consequences.

The Anthropocene, a proposed geological epoch dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems, remains a subject of intense debate among stratigraphers. While some argue the epoch should begin with the Industrial Revolution in the late 18th century, others point to the mid-20th century 'Great Acceleration' characterized by nuclear testing and plastic proliferation. This latter period is marked by the presence of radioactive isotopes in the soil, providing a clear 'golden spike' in the geological record. Critics, however, suggest that formalizing the Anthropocene may be premature, as the long-term lithostratigraphic evidence is still being deposited.

In the realm of international relations, the 'Thucydides Trap' describes the inherent friction that occurs when a rising power threatens to displace an established hegemon. Historically, this structural stress has often culminated in systemic war, as seen in the rivalry between Sparta and Athens. Modern scholars apply this lens to the current US-China dynamic, emphasizing that conflict is not inevitable but requires meticulous diplomacy to avoid. The challenge lies in the 'security dilemma,' where one nation's defensive measures are perceived as offensive threats by the other, triggering a cycle of escalation. To mitigate this, institutional frameworks and economic interdependence are often cited as crucial stabilizing factors.

The concept of 'Deep Time,' first articulated by James Hutton in the 18th century, fundamentally altered the human perception of history. Before this geological revolution, the Earth was widely believed to be only a few thousand years old. Hutton’s observations of unconformities—layers of rock meeting at odd angles—suggested a cycle of erosion and sedimentation that required millions, if not billions, of years. This paradigm shift was essential for the development of Darwin’s theory of evolution, as it provided the vast timescales necessary for gradual natural selection. Despite its scientific importance, the human brain struggles to comprehend such scales, often defaulting to 'anthropocentric' timelines.

Cognitive dissonance occurs when an individual holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values simultaneously. This mental discomfort leads to an unconscious drive to resolve the inconsistency. According to Leon Festinger's theory, people typically reduce dissonance by changing their attitudes, beliefs, or behaviors, rather than admitting a mistake. For instance, a smoker who knows that tobacco is harmful might rationalize the habit by claiming it reduces stress. This mechanism serves to protect the individual's self-esteem but can also hinder personal growth by reinforcing denial. Understanding this phenomenon is critical in fields ranging from marketing to psychotherapy.

questions.question: The discovery of the ancient scroll was so significant that it required the immediate attention of the world's leading archaeologists.

options.A: Leading archaeologists were already looking for the scroll when it was finally discovered.
options.B: The ancient scroll was found by a group of archaeologists who were well-known throughout the world.
options.C: Because the found scroll was of such great importance, top archaeologists from around the globe had to examine it right away.
options.D: The scroll was not as significant as the archaeologists had originally thought.
options.E: Only the world's leading archaeologists were allowed to see the scroll after its discovery.

questions.question: It was only after several failed attempts that the engineering team finally succeeded in stabilizing the bridge's foundation.

options.A: The engineering team stabilized the bridge's foundation on their first try.
options.B: The bridge's foundation could not be stabilized despite the engineers' many efforts.
options.C: After trying and failing a number of times, the engineers eventually managed to make the bridge's foundation stable.
options.D: Several attempts were made to stabilize the foundation, but the engineering team was unsuccessful.
options.E: The engineers would have succeeded sooner if they hadn't failed in their initial attempts.

questions.question: The placebo effect is a fascinating psychological phenomenon where a patient experiences a real improvement in their condition after receiving a 'dummy' treatment. --- Researchers believe that the patient's expectation of recovery triggers the release of endorphins and other natural chemicals in the brain. This suggests that the mind plays a much larger role in physical healing than previously understood.

options.A: However, most doctors refuse to use placebos in their clinical practice.
options.B: This effect occurs even though the treatment has no active medicinal ingredients.
options.C: Placebos are often more expensive than the actual drugs being tested.
options.D: The history of the placebo effect dates back to ancient times.
options.E: Clinical trials often fail because the placebo group shows no response.

questions.question: Urban sprawl refers to the unrestricted expansion of housing, commercial development, and roads over large expanses of land. --- This often results in increased reliance on automobiles, as public transport becomes less efficient in low-density areas. Consequently, urban sprawl is frequently linked to higher carbon emissions and the loss of agricultural land.

options.A: City centers are becoming more crowded as people move away from the suburbs.
options.B: Many people prefer living in the city because of the proximity to work.
options.C: It typically involves the transformation of rural or undeveloped land into suburban areas.
options.D: Governments are trying to encourage urban sprawl to solve the housing crisis.
options.E: The cost of living in rural areas is significantly higher than in the city.

questions.question: The concept of 'Soft Power' was coined by Joseph Nye to describe a nation's ability to influence others through attraction rather than coercion. --- This includes the export of culture, political values, and foreign policies that are seen as legitimate. In contrast, 'Hard Power' relies on military force or economic sanctions to achieve national goals.

options.A: Military spending is the most common way to increase soft power.
options.B: Nye argued that soft power is no longer relevant in the modern world.
options.C: It is often achieved through the use of 'carrots' and 'sticks'.
options.D: Instead of using threats, a country leverages its cultural and ideological appeal.
options.E: Soft power is only effective if a country also has a strong military.

questions.question: Bees play a critical role in the global ecosystem as primary pollinators for many crops. --- Without them, the production of many fruits, vegetables, and nuts would decline sharply. This would not only lead to food shortages but also cause significant economic disruption in the agricultural sector.

options.A: Honey production is the most profitable part of the bee industry.
options.B: In fact, nearly one-third of the food we consume depends on pollination by bees.
options.C: There are many different species of bees found all over the world.
options.D: Bees are known for their complex social structures within the hive.
options.E: Pesticides have been linked to the decline of bee populations in some regions.

questions.question: (I) Quantum computing represents a shift in how information is processed, using qubits instead of bits. (II) Traditional computers use binary code, which consists of ones and zeros. (III) These qubits allow for calculations to be performed simultaneously, offering immense speed. (IV) However, quantum computers are extremely sensitive to their environment and require near-absolute zero temperatures. (V) Many people still prefer to use laptops for their daily work because they are portable.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) The Renaissance was a period of great cultural and artistic change that began in Italy. (II) It was marked by a renewed interest in the classical philosophy and art of Greece and Rome. (III) Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo are often cited as the most influential figures of this era. (IV) Modern art movements like Cubism and Surrealism challenged these traditional forms. (V) The invention of the printing press also played a vital role in spreading Renaissance ideas across Europe.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Dark matter is an invisible substance that is thought to make up about 27% of the universe. (II) It does not emit, absorb, or reflect light, making it incredibly difficult to detect directly. (III) Scientists infer its existence through its gravitational effects on visible matter. (IV) Black holes are regions of space where gravity is so strong that nothing can escape. (V) Understanding dark matter is one of the most significant challenges in modern astrophysics.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Microplastics are tiny plastic particles less than five millimeters in diameter. (II) They have been found in the deepest parts of the ocean and even in human blood. (III) Most plastics are made from petroleum-based chemicals that are not biodegradable. (IV) These particles enter the food chain when they are ingested by marine life. (V) The long-term health effects of microplastic exposure are still being studied by researchers.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Epigenetics is the study of how behaviors and environment can cause changes that affect the way genes work. (II) Unlike genetic changes, epigenetic changes are reversible and do not change the DNA sequence. (III) These changes can be passed down to future generations, influencing their health. (IV) Mendel’s experiments with pea plants laid the foundation for modern genetics. (V) Factors such as diet, exercise, and stress are known to trigger epigenetic modifications.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V
